query: current use of thalidomide as an immunomodulatory drug

retrieved_docs.title: Thalidomide -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Thalidomide is a medication used to manage and treat advanced leprosy and multiple myeloma, and various other solid and hematologic malignancies. It is in the immunomodulatory class of medications. This activity reviews the indication, action, and contraindications for thalidomide as a valuable agent in the treatment of leprosy, multiple myeloma, and other malignancies when applicable. This activity will highlight the mechanism of action, adverse event profile, and other key factors pertinent for interprofessional team members managing the care of patients with leprosy, multiple myeloma, and other malignancies and their related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: Thalidomide -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Historically, thalidomide is best known for its introduction during the 1950s as a sedative and antiemetic marketed to women to treat pregnancy-related morning sickness. [1] During this time, the compound quickly developed its infamous reputation as one of the most potent teratogenic substances ever promoted, as approximately 10,000 children were born with profound limb and internal organ abnormalities, later referred to as the "children of thalidomide." The compound was then banned until later research demonstrated novel applications to its poorly understood mechanism of action. In the 1990s, the drug was repurposed, as the FDA approved it to treat conditions such as erythema nodosum leprosum. This repurposing generated further research into understanding the drug's mechanisms and ultimately uncovered its effective anti-inflammatory, immunomodulatory and anti-angiogenic activities. [2] Such properties have led to further FDA approval for the treatment of multiple myeloma. [3] Thalidomide also has an orphan designation for the treatment for the following:

retrieved_docs.title: Thalidomide -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the unique molecular composition of thalidomide and how this affects the drug’s therapeutic and adverse effects. Identify the most common adverse effects associated with thalidomide therapy. Summarize proper treatment and responses to thalidomide toxicity. Explain the importance of the interprofessional healthcare team monitoring patients taking thalidomide, especially females of reproductive age. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Leprosy -- Treatment / Management -- Treatment of Immunologic Reactions
retrieved_docs.content: Thalidomide is considered an extremely viable option to treat type 2 reactions, but it could affect women capable of childbearing. [89] The efficiency of thalidomide in treatment is the primary reason as to why it has not been outlawed. The initial dose of 300 to 400 mg daily controls the reaction in the body within 48 hours. After treatment has begun, the dose should be lowered to maintenance concentration around 100 mg daily, every couple of months. In attempts to control ENL (erythema nodosum leprosum), thalidomide treatment can proceed for a number of years. Neuropathy advancement should indicate immediately stopping treatment. Alternative Agents

retrieved_docs.title: Thalidomide -- Toxicity
retrieved_docs.content: Peripheral neuropathy Mild: Decrease the thalidomide dose by 50% Moderate: Withhold thalidomide therapy until there is a resolution of toxicity and then restart at a 50% reduction Severe: Discontinue thalidomide therapy

retrieved_docs.title: Thalidomide -- Administration
retrieved_docs.content: Dosing of thalidomide is usually via the oral route with various strengths up to 200 mg tablets for all known treatment indications. [1] [9] However, some sources question the bioavailability of thalidomide after oral administration due to its spontaneous hydrolysis and incomplete absorption. [10] [11] [9] Studies of intravenous administration demonstrate similar bioavailability to that of oral, while studies of other dosage forms could not be found. [9] Thus, the development of novel drug delivery systems in the form of biodegradable thalidomide implants has been a topic of research. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Thalidomide -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Previous hypersensitivity reactions represent the final contraindication to thalidomide usage. Patients who previously displayed severe adverse events such as Stevens-Johnson syndrome or type 1 hypersensitivity reaction to this drug should no longer be considered candidates for therapy. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Thalidomide -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The most common adverse effects of thalidomide therapy include constipation, sedation, fatigue, and neuropathy. [14] Such effects are related to therapeutic dosage and duration and can affect patient compliance and therapeutic benefits. More serious effects include increased thrombus formation and hypersensitivity reactions. To avoid missed indications of toxicity, healthcare providers at all levels must monitor any related symptoms and advise appropriate alterations to therapy – especially when therapy involves combined treatment with dexamethasone. [22] [Level 1]

retrieved_docs.title: Thalidomide -- Toxicity
retrieved_docs.content: Further steps for addressing thalidomide toxicity involve enrolling both physician and patient into the System for Thalidomide Education and Prescribing Safety (S.T.E.P.S) program. [14] [21] This program seeks to reduce misuse of thalidomide and thus mitigate adverse events (especially teratogenicity) via a three-pronged approach involving: access control, education of healthcare providers, pharmacists, and patients, and monitoring of compliance.

retrieved_docs.title: Thalidomide -- Toxicity
retrieved_docs.content: There is no indicated antidote for thalidomide overdose. Thalidomide toxicity directly correlates with drug dosage and duration of therapy. [14] Patients taking 200 mg or less appear to have good therapeutic outcomes without evident toxicity, while dosages exceeding 400 mg/day almost always lead to toxicity-related symptoms. The most common symptoms related to thalidomide toxicity include constipation, sedation, fatigue, and neuropathy. Therapy exceeding six months is associated with an increase in neuropathy and hypothyroidism related to thalidomide toxicity. However, the incidence of constipation and sedation seems to decrease with the continued duration of therapy. Thalidomide toxicity also seems to increase when used in conjunction with dexamethasone or other chemotherapy drugs. Of those receiving thalidomide and dexamethasone for multiple myeloma treatment, approximately 10% will discontinue the treatment due to toxicity-related effects. [14] [22] [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Thalidomide -- Toxicity
retrieved_docs.content: Somnolence Instruct patients to administer their total daily dose before going to bed Patients should avoid driving or operating machinery In cases of stupor or coma, suspend thalidomide therapy until toxicity resolves, then restart treatment at a 50% dose

retrieved_docs.title: Thalidomide -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: The use of thalidomide correlates with significant contraindications that physicians should consider before prescribing this compound. In particular, the FDA Black Box Warning involves embryo-fetal toxicity. [14] If administered to a pregnant patient, thalidomide can cause severe congenital defects or death. Thus, clinicians must avoid using thalidomide in pregnant women or women who may become pregnant while taking the drug. Before initiating treatment with any amount of this drug, pregnancy must be excluded with two negative pregnancy tests and subsequently prevented with two reliable contraceptive methods. Prescribers of thalidomide must also receive certification with the THALOMID REMS program through enrollment and compliance with the REMS requirements. [15]

retrieved_docs.content: Steps for addressing symptoms of thalidomide-related toxicity are listed below.

retrieved_docs.title: Thalidomide -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: Proper monitoring of patients treated with thalidomide is essential to ensuring a safe and efficacious treatment, as this drug is related to numerous, potentially severe, adverse events and reactions. [1] Despite questionable bioavailability via gastrointestinal absorption, almost all thalidomide therapy is delivered PO at dosages of 50-200 mg once daily. [10] [20] Thalidomide is absorbed slowly, with peak concentrations occurring within 2 to 5 hours after ingestion. [20] Due to spontaneous hydrolysis at physiologic pH, >90% of thalidomide is renally excreted, with <5% of the drug being unchanged. The half-life of thalidomide is 5 to 7 hours, and toxicities may develop with increased dosage and duration of therapy. [20] [14] The most common symptoms related to thalidomide toxicity include constipation, sedation, fatigue, and neuropathy. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1846
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic glucocorticoids constitute a class of corticosteroid drugs that exert broad anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive actions throughout the body. Both prednison and rimexolone are individual agents that belong to this systemic glucocorticoid class, sharing the common pharmacologic properties of glucocorticoid receptor activation, suppression of cytokine production, and modulation of immune cell function. Recognizing prednison and rimexolone as members of the systemic glucocorticoid group helps clinicians understand their therapeutic indications, dosing considerations, and potential side‑effect profiles, which are characteristic of systemic glucocorticoid therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1964
retrieved_docs.content: Immunosuppressants are a subgroup of immunologic factors that encompass a wide range of agents used to modulate the immune response. Within this class, mycophenolate and its pro‑drug mycophenolate mofetil (including the formulation hydrochlorid mykofenolátu mofetilu) are recognized as important immunosuppressive drugs. Other members of the immunosuppressant family include the monoclonal antibodies basiliximab and daclizumab, the calcineurin inhibitor cyclosporine, the mTOR inhibitor everolimus, and the compound elipticiny. Key representative substances of this therapeutic group are mycophenolic acid (kyselina mykofenolová) and tacrolimus (takrolimus), which exemplify the pharmacologic mechanisms underlying immunosuppression. Together, these agents illustrate the diversity of immunosuppressive therapies that are employed to prevent graft rejection, treat autoimmune disorders, and manage other conditions requiring immune modulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 381
retrieved_docs.content: Immunosuppressants comprise a diverse group of agents that are used to dampen immune activity in settings such as organ transplantation, autoimmune disorders, and inflammatory diseases. This class includes inhibitory interleukin agents, the antimetabolite mycophenolate (also known as mycophenolic acid) and its pro‑drug mycophenolate mofetil (including its hydrochloride form), as well as azaserin, basiliximab, cyclosporine, everolimus, gliotoxin, muromonab‑CD3, and tacrolimus. Mycophenolate and mycophenolic acid function by blocking purine synthesis in lymphocytes, whereas cyclosporine and tacrolimus inhibit calcineurin, preventing activation of T‑cell transcription factors. Everolimus exerts its effect through inhibition of the mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR) pathway, limiting cell proliferation. Basiliximab and muromonab‑CD3 are monoclonal antibodies that target specific surface receptors on activated T cells, providing targeted immunosuppression. Gliotoxin, a fungal toxin, also possesses immunosuppressive properties through interference with cytokine signaling. Collectively, these agents illustrate the multiple mechanistic pathways—interleukin inhibition, antimetabolite activity, calcineurin blockade, mTOR inhibition, and monoclonal antibody‑mediated receptor blockade—by which immunosuppressants achieve therapeutic immune modulation, underscoring their central role in contemporary patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: Indomethacin is identified as the parent compound for the immunomodulatory drug class, meaning that the immunomodulatory agents have indomethacin as their “has parent” relationship. This connection indicates that immunomodulatory drugs are derived from or chemically related to indomethacin, highlighting a pharmacological lineage that can inform understanding of their mechanisms and potential therapeutic applications.

retrieved_docs.content: التصلب اللويحي (multiple sclerosis) may be treated with triamcinolon, a drug that belongs to the class of systemic glucocorticoids. Systemic glucocorticoids, represented by both triamcinolon and rimexolone, share anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive properties and are employed in the management of various inflammatory and autoimmune disorders. Recognizing that triamcinolon and rimexolone are members of the systemic glucocorticoid group informs clinicians in selecting appropriate steroid therapy for conditions such as multiple sclerosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 725
retrieved_docs.content: Dexamethasone belongs to the class of hormonal antitumor agents and is also classified as an immunosuppressant. In pharmaceutical preparations, dexamethasone is combined with the antibiotics neomycin and polymyxin, forming a product whose active ingredients are neomycin + polymyxin + dexamethasone. A wide range of other substances are identified as immunosuppressants, including antilymphocyte serum, azaserin, cyclosporins, daclizumab, everolimus, gliotoxin, mycophenolic acid (kyselina mykofenolová), koformycin, muromonab‑CD3, and tacrolimus (takrolimus). Inhibitory interleukin is also considered a subclass within the immunosuppressant category. Together, these agents represent diverse mechanisms for suppressing immune activity, ranging from corticosteroid action (dexamethasone) to monoclonal antibodies (daclizumab, muromonab‑CD3), calcineurin inhibitors (cyclosporins, tacrolimus), mTOR inhibitors (everolimus), and other biochemical inhibitors, underscoring their relevance in transplant medicine, autoimmune disease management, and certain oncologic therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2739
retrieved_docs.content: Immunologické faktory constitute a broader category that includes immunosuppressants, a class of agents used to dampen immune activity. Immunosuppressants themselves comprise a diverse group of pharmacologic and biologic products, including antilymfocytární sérum, azaserin, basiliximab, elipticiny, everolimus, as well as specific drugs such as koformycin, kyselina mykofenolová (mycophenolic acid) and takrolimus. Within this hierarchy, inhibitory interleukinu is identified as a child concept of immunosuppressants, reflecting its role in specifically inhibiting interleukin‑mediated immune pathways. This organized relationship underscores how various immunosuppressive therapies are interconnected under the umbrella of immunological factors, facilitating a clearer understanding for clinicians when selecting agents for graft rejection prophylaxis, autoimmune disease management, and other conditions requiring immune modulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1722
retrieved_docs.content: Immunosuppressants are a class of immunologic factors that act to dampen or inhibit the activity of the immune system. Within this class, several agents are identified as direct “children,” including inhibitory interleukin agents, mycophenolate, and mycophenolate mofetil (mykofenolát mofetil). A broad range of specific drugs and biologic preparations are also classified as immunosuppressants: antilymphocyte serum, cyclosporine (cyklosporiny), daclizumab, everolimus, gliotoxin, and the hydrochloride form of mycophenolate mofetil (hydrochlorid mykofenolátu mofetilu). Additional individual compounds that belong to the immunosuppressant group are mycophenolic acid (kyselina mykofenolová), koformycin, muromonab‑CD3, paramethasone, and tacrolimus (takrolimus). Collectively, these agents are employed to prevent graft rejection in organ transplantation, to treat autoimmune disorders, and to manage other conditions where suppression of immune activity is therapeutically desirable. Their shared classification under immunosuppressants underscores a common mechanism of reducing immune‑mediated damage while highlighting the diversity of chemical structures and clinical applications within the group.
